segments.text: In 2020, Iceland and the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network jointly published a case series documenting five people who developed jaundice from a supplement. Not a drug. A supplement. One that a million people search for every month.

Ashwagandha. The sleepy adaptogen your wellness influencer told you to take for stress. Denmark banned it in 2023. The NIH's LiverTox database lists it as a known hepatotoxic herb. And almost nobody selling it mentions that.
And the thing is — the evidence for some of what ashwagandha does is real. That's what makes this complicated.
The signal exists. It is narrower than claimed, it is form-dependent, and there is a hepatotoxicity file that you will not find in the supplement aisle. That's the honest summary of 500-plus studies.
So let's work through it. What does the market look like first — because the scale here is part of the story.

Ashwagandha is the most-searched supplement in the adaptogen category. Roughly one million queries per month in English alone. The wellness industry has built a $1.5 billion global market on it. Amazon lists over two thousand products containing it.
And what are those products promising?

Stress destroyer. Testosterone booster. Cognitive enhancer. Sleep fix. The marketing speaks in absolutes. The peer-reviewed literature speaks in population-specific effects from proprietary extracts under disclosed industry funding.
Those are very different things.
Very different. And the gap between them is where most supplement users are operating in the dark.
Start with the extract problem — because this is the thing that almost no one explains when they cite the clinical trials.

When supplement brands say they have 24 clinical trials behind their product, they are almost always citing trials on KSM-66 — a root-only extract manufactured by Ixoreal Biomed, an Indian ingredient company. Sensoril, from Natreon Inc., has 10-plus trials. Shoden, from Arjuna Natural, is used in more recent studies.
So the evidence base isn't for ashwagandha. It's for three specific branded extracts.

Exactly. The active constituents — withanolides, withanosides, alkaloids — vary enormously by plant part, extraction method, and standardization. KSM-66 is standardized to at least five percent withanolides from root only. Shoden is standardized to thirty-five percent withanolide glycosides. Generic root powder at your pharmacy may have one to two percent — or none.
Meaning a study on KSM-66 tells you almost nothing about the dollar-a-bottle product.

Nothing. And there's a second layer to this: the manufacturers funded most of the supporting research. Chandrasekhar et al., 2012, in the Indian Journal of Psychological Medicine — KSM-66 trial. Lopresti, 2019 — Shoden manufacturer funded the study. The researcher discloses it, in the paper. The people citing the conclusions frequently omit that part.
Adrian Lopresti, Murdoch University. He's the most-cited modern name in ashwagandha RCTs.
He is. And his disclosures are in his papers. The problem isn't the researcher — the problem is how those papers get cited downstream, stripped of context.
Okay. So what actually replicates? What's the real signal?
Three outcomes appear consistently across the best-designed trials. Cortisol. Anxiety. Sleep.
Walk me through the cortisol finding.

Chandrasekhar, Kapoor, and Anishetty ran a 60-day randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 64 chronically stressed adults. The ashwagandha group received 300 milligrams of KSM-66 twice daily. Serum cortisol dropped by 27.9% compared to placebo — p equals 0.0006. The Perceived Stress Scale dropped 44% versus 5.5% for placebo.
That's a large effect.

It is. Chandrasekhar et al. write: "Serum cortisol levels were substantially reduced in the ashwagandha group relative to the placebo group, p equals 0.0006." Lopresti replicated the cortisol finding in 2019 using Shoden extract, 240 milligrams per day, 60 days, 60 stressed adults. Morning cortisol significantly lower. p less than 0.001. Funded by Arjuna Natural — and Lopresti says so in the disclosures.
The disclosure is there if you look for it.

It's there. The 2019 Lopresti study also measured anxiety via the Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale. Statistically significant reduction versus placebo — p equals 0.040. The DASS-21 trended toward significance at p equals 0.096, but did not cross the threshold.
So anxiety — real effect, moderate, in stressed adults. Not what the marketing implies.

The NCCIH — the National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health — reviewed the evidence in 2023 and wrote: "Research shows that some ashwagandha preparations may be effective for insomnia and stress. However, evidence is unclear about their effects on anxiety." That's the U.S. government's official synthesis.
Unclear on anxiety. What about sleep — where's that standing?

Langade, Kanchi, Salve et al. ran a 10-week RCT — 150 subjects — using Shoden, standardized to 21 milligrams of withanolide glycosides per 120 milligrams daily. Sleep onset latency: 29 minutes in the ashwagandha group versus 33.9 minutes for placebo, p equals 0.019. Sleep efficiency: 83.5% versus 79.7%, p less than 0.001.
That's the largest ashwagandha sleep trial we have?

150 subjects is the largest well-designed one. Langade et al. write: "Ashwagandha root extract significantly improved sleep quality, sleep onset latency, and sleep efficiency in adults with insomnia compared to placebo." The effect is real. It's also specific — 150 subjects, one extract, one manufacturer.
What about athletic performance — because that's a big category for this supplement.

Bonilla, Moreno, Gho et al. published a Bayesian meta-analysis in 2021 in Sports — that's an MDPI journal — covering six studies and seven effect sizes for VO2 max. Ashwagandha showed a statistically significant positive effect on cardiorespiratory fitness. The effect size was real but modest. Meaningful for recreational athletes. Not the performance revolution the sports supplement market implies.
And testosterone — because I guarantee that's the number-one reason men are buying this.

Lopresti, Drummond, and Smith ran a 16-week crossover RCT — 57 subjects, overweight men aged 40 to 70, mild fatigue symptoms — using Shoden. Testosterone increased by 14.7% more than placebo, p equals 0.010. DHEA-S increased 18% more than placebo.
14.7%. That's the number floating around fitness circles.

It is. And it requires that exact population — overweight, aging, mildly fatigued — that exact extract, Shoden at 21 milligrams of withanolide glycosides daily, and a 16-week duration. Lopresti et al. write: "Ashwagandha was associated with a 14.7% greater increase in testosterone compared to placebo in aging, overweight males with mild fatigue symptoms." It does not generalize to young, lean, non-fatigued men.
Which is exactly the population being sold to.
The 24-year-old watching a fitness influencer is not the 57-subject population in Lopresti's RCT. That's the gap.
What about the cognitive enhancement claims — because those are everywhere now.

Pingali, Pilli, and Fatima ran a 6-month trial using Sensoril. 20 young healthy males. The ashwagandha group showed improvements in reaction time, digit vigilance, memory tasks. Pingali et al. write: "Sensoril ashwagandha extract showed improvements in psychomotor and cognitive performance versus placebo in a 6-month trial of 20 healthy male volunteers."
20 subjects. One research group. One extract.

And the Alzheimer's Drug Discovery Foundation's Cognitive Vitality program reviewed the full evidence and rated ashwagandha as having "limited" evidence for cognitive benefit — one tier below promising — citing small study sizes and methodological limitations. Independent replication is thin.
So the cognition story is: preliminary, narrow, not ready for the broad claim.

Correct. Same with libido and sexual function. Small trials, lack of placebo controls, non-standardized extracts. The testosterone finding is real — but testosterone modulation in a narrow clinical population is not a universal aphrodisiac.
Now the part that most supplement blogs won't touch. The liver.

In 2020, Björnsson and colleagues published a case series in Liver International — a peer-reviewed gastroenterology journal — documenting five cases of ashwagandha-induced liver injury. Three cases from Iceland, collected in 2017 and 2018. Two from the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network.
What did those cases look like clinically?

Mean age 43, range 21 to 62. All five developed jaundice two to twelve weeks after starting ashwagandha. Liver injury was cholestatic or mixed pattern. One biopsy showed acute cholestatic hepatitis. Pruritus and hyperbilirubinemia persisted five to twenty weeks after stopping the supplement.
Five to twenty weeks of symptoms after stopping.

None developed liver failure. Liver tests normalized in one to five months. Björnsson et al. write: "Five cases of liver injury attributed to ashwagandha-containing supplements were identified — three in Iceland and two from DILIN. All patients developed jaundice two to twelve weeks post-initiation. Liver injury was cholestatic or mixed with prolonged pruritus and hyperbilirubinemia."
And where does that sit officially now?

The NIH's LiverTox database — which tracks drug-induced liver injury — lists ashwagandha as a known hepatotoxic herb. Their 2023 entry states: "Ashwagandha has been implicated in rare instances of clinically apparent liver injury, which is typically cholestatic or mixed in pattern."
Denmark banned it in April 2023.

The Danish Veterinary and Food Administration cited a 2020 risk assessment by the Technical University of Denmark — concerns about hormonal disruption and potential reproductive effects at physiological doses. The abortion risk claim has been contested by independent researchers. The liver injury finding is harder to dismiss.
Low incidence applied to one-million-monthly-searches means thousands of people per year.

And most won't associate unexplained jaundice with the stress supplement they started a month earlier. The plain statement: if you develop fatigue, dark urine, yellowing of the skin or eyes, or upper-right abdominal discomfort while using ashwagandha — stop immediately and consult a physician. This is a documented adverse event pattern, not a theoretical risk.
There's also a quality problem on top of all of this.

ConsumerLab — an independent supplement testing organization — found that only 38% of ashwagandha products it reviewed passed testing for quality, accurate labeling, and contamination limits. ConsumerLab writes: "Only 38% of ashwagandha products reviewed passed testing for quality, label accuracy, and contamination limits."
So the majority of products on the shelf fail independent testing.

And ashwagandha root powder has documented potential for heavy metal bioaccumulation. A 2010 review of medicinal plants found ashwagandha showing very high metal bioaccumulation relative to other plants examined. Third-party certification from NSF International, USP, or ConsumerLab is the minimum floor.
So let's do the honest accounting. What can we actually say?

We can say: KSM-66 root extract at 300 to 600 milligrams per day for eight to twelve weeks reduces serum cortisol by approximately 27.9% relative to placebo in chronically stressed adults — based on a peer-reviewed RCT with disclosed industry funding.
We can say: Shoden extract at 120 to 240 milligrams per day improves sleep onset latency, sleep efficiency, and non-restorative sleep in adults with insomnia, based on a 10-week, 150-person RCT.

We can say: Shoden at 21 milligrams of withanolide glycosides daily increases testosterone by approximately 14.7% more than placebo — in overweight, aging men with mild fatigue. Specific population. Specific product. Specific duration.
We can say: cholestatic liver injury is a documented adverse event, captured in peer-reviewed case series from Iceland and the U.S. DILIN, with onset two to twelve weeks post-initiation.
And what can't we say?
We can't say that ashwagandha as a category reliably produces any of these effects. Extract, dose, and population all determine whether an effect exists.
We can't say that cognitive enhancement claims for healthy young adults are well-supported. The evidence is preliminary, from small trials, in narrow populations.

We can't say that long-term safety beyond twelve weeks is established. The longest well-designed trial runs sixteen weeks. The Danish Technical University flagged hormonal concerns at chronic doses. The liver injury cases include durations as short as two weeks.
And we can't say the product you're buying matches the extract that was tested.
Given ConsumerLab's 38% pass rate — more likely than not, it doesn't.
The supplement industry has built an enormous market on a real but narrow signal — and then extrapolated it into a category, a lifestyle, an identity.

The honest read of 500-plus studies is not "it works." It is: for specific extracts, at specific doses, in specific populations, it produces measurable effects on cortisol and sleep. The evidence for liver toxicity is real. The evidence for most marketing claims is not.

The question worth sitting with: if a pharmaceutical compound had a hepatotoxicity case series, an uncertain long-term safety profile, and a 38% product quality pass rate — would we treat the marketing claims with the same latitude we give to supplements?
If sleep is the signal you're actually chasing, the transmission on magnesium covers a compound with a different funding profile and a longer independent evidence trail.
